"𝗜𝘁'𝘀 𝗷𝘂𝘀𝘁 𝗮𝗻𝗼𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗿 𝗔𝗜 𝘄𝗿𝗮𝗽𝗽𝗲𝗿."

This is becoming one of the laziest takes in software.

Calling every AI product a wrapper is like calling a distributed system "𝗷𝘂𝘀𝘁 𝘀𝗼𝗺𝗲 𝘀𝗲𝗿𝘃𝗲𝗿𝘀."

The hard part isn't calling an LLM.

The hard part is everything around it.

I didn't build ⚡ 𝗔𝗲𝗴𝗶𝘀 because I wanted another chatbot.

I built it because I wanted to answer one question:

𝗖𝗮𝗻 𝗮𝗻 𝗔𝗜 𝗶𝗻𝘃𝗲𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗴𝗮𝘁𝗲 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗱𝘂𝗰𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝘀𝘆𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗺𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝘄𝗮𝘆 𝗮 𝘀𝗲𝗻𝗶𝗼𝗿 𝗲𝗻𝗴𝗶𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗿 𝘄𝗼𝘂𝗹𝗱?

That means correlating multiple data sources.

Planning investigations instead of randomly calling tools.

Executing generated code safely.

Streaming every reasoning step.

And doing all of that without compromising production security.

You don't learn that by chaining prompts together.

You learn it by building the entire system.

❌ No "AI agent" framework doing the heavy lifting.
❌ No black-box orchestration.
✅ Just raw systems engineering around LLMs.

Here's what actually powers Aegis:

• 🧠 𝗡𝗮𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗮𝗹 𝗟𝗮𝗻𝗴𝘂𝗮𝗴𝗲 → 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗱𝘂𝗰𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗤𝘂𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗲𝘀 - Translate plain English into SQL, CloudWatch Logs Insights, and API calls.
• 🔗 𝗠𝘂𝗹𝘁𝗶-𝗦𝗼𝘂𝗿𝗰𝗲 𝗖𝗼𝗿𝗿𝗲𝗹𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 - Connect PostgreSQL, CloudWatch Logs, external APIs, and other data sources into a single investigation.
• 🔍 𝗦𝘁𝗿𝘂𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲𝗱 𝗜𝗻𝘃𝗲𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗴𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗘𝗻𝗴𝗶𝗻𝗲 - Every incident follows 𝗦𝗰𝗼𝗽𝗲 → 𝗘𝘅𝗽𝗹𝗼𝗿𝗲 → 𝗖𝗼𝗹𝗹𝗲𝗰𝘁 → 𝗔𝗻𝗮𝗹𝘆𝘇𝗲 instead of letting the LLM wander aimlessly.
• 🔒 𝗗𝗲𝗳𝗲𝗻𝘀𝗲-𝗶𝗻-𝗗𝗲𝗽𝘁𝗵 𝗦𝗮𝗻𝗱𝗯𝗼𝘅 - AI-generated Python runs inside Docker, NSJail, seccomp, and application-level permission guards with zero network access.
• ⚡ 𝗥𝗲𝗮𝗹-𝗧𝗶𝗺𝗲 𝗧𝗿𝗮𝗻𝘀𝗽𝗮𝗿𝗲𝗻𝗰𝘆 - Every tool call, query, chart, and reasoning step streams live. No mysterious "thinking..." spinner.
• 🔌 𝗗𝗲𝗽𝗹𝗼𝘆 𝗔𝗻𝘆𝘄𝗵𝗲𝗿𝗲 - Run the same engine as a FastAPI chatbot or an MCP server with zero architectural changes.

The prompt isn't the product.

The architecture is.

I'm continuing my engineering deep-dive series where I document the architecture, trade-offs, and design decisions behind the systems I build.

No AI hype.

Just engineering.

📖 𝗥𝗲𝗮𝗱 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗳𝘂𝗹𝗹 𝗔𝗲𝗴𝗶𝘀 𝗲𝗻𝗴𝗶𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗱𝗲𝗲𝗽 𝗱𝗶𝘃𝗲:

https://lnkd.in/gBbnUAzs

𝗪𝗵𝗮𝘁'𝘀 𝗼𝗻𝗲 𝗲𝗻𝗴𝗶𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗰𝗵𝗮𝗹𝗹𝗲𝗻𝗴𝗲 𝗶𝗻 𝗔𝗜 𝘁𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗸 𝗴𝗲𝘁𝘀 𝗺𝗮𝘀𝘀𝗶𝘃𝗲𝗹𝘆 𝘂𝗻𝗱𝗲𝗿𝗲𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗺𝗮𝘁𝗲𝗱? I'd love to hear your take.

#PlatformEngineering #SoftwareArchitecture #DistributedSystems #SystemDesign #CloudEngineering #BackendDevelopment #AIEngineering #LLM #DeveloperTools #BuildInPublic

"𝗡𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗿 𝗿𝗲𝗶𝗻𝘃𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝘄𝗵𝗲𝗲𝗹."

This is the most 𝘁𝗼𝘅𝗶𝗰 advice fed to developers today.

If you only ever glue together pre-packaged APIs and rely on managed platforms, your skills will plateau. Fast. You don't become a better architect by configuring someone else's black box.

I didn't build 🚀 𝗦𝘁𝗮𝗿𝗱𝘂𝘀𝘁 because I actually need my own personal container provisioning engine.

I built it because it’s a brutally hard problem to solve.

I wanted to learn exactly how to synchronize unpredictable async tasks across distributed systems, and figure out how to automatically scale infrastructure up and down based purely on real-time demand. You don't learn that by reading AWS whitepapers. You learn it by building it.

❌ No Kubernetes overkill.
❌ No black-box magic.
✅ Just raw Platform Engineering.

Here is what it actually takes under the hood when you rip off the training wheels:

• 🏗️ 𝗜𝗻𝗳𝗿𝗮𝘀𝘁𝗿𝘂𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲 𝗮𝘀 𝗖𝗼𝗱𝗲 - 100% Pulumi (because Terraform is aging poorly).
• ⚙️ 𝗔𝘀𝘆𝗻𝗰 𝗠𝗶𝗰𝗿𝗼𝘀𝗲𝗿𝘃𝗶𝗰𝗲𝘀 - RabbitMQ powered workers orchestrating builds and Spot instance recovery.
• ☁️ Amazon Web Services (AWS) 𝗙𝗮𝗿𝗴𝗮𝘁𝗲 & 𝗘𝗖𝗥 - Abstracted behind a Strategy pattern so we aren't locked into Jeff Bezos' ecosystem forever.
• ⚡ 𝗥𝗲𝗮𝗹-𝗧𝗶𝗺𝗲 𝗘𝘃𝗲𝗿𝘆𝘁𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴 - Live browser SSH and streaming build logs via Redis pub/sub.

"Reinventing the wheel" isn't a waste of time. It's how you learn to build a better engine.

I’m starting a new series of engineering deep-dives where I document the architecture, the bottlenecks, and the trade-offs of the systems I build.

No surface-level showcases. Just raw platform engineering.

📖 𝗥𝗲𝗮𝗱 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗳𝘂𝗹𝗹 𝗦𝘁𝗮𝗿𝗱𝘂𝘀𝘁 𝗲𝗻𝗴𝗶𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗱𝗲𝗲𝗽-𝗱𝗶𝘃𝗲 𝗵𝗲𝗿𝗲: https://lnkd.in/gw6b7M-F

What is the most "unnecessarily complex" system you've built just to see how it works under the hood? Let’s argue in the comments. 👇

#PlatformEngineering #DistributedSystems #CloudComputing #SoftwareArchitecture #DevOps

𝗛𝗢𝗧 𝗧𝗔𝗞𝗘:

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝘀𝗼𝗳𝘁𝘄𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝗶𝗻𝗱𝘂𝘀𝘁𝗿𝘆 𝗶𝘀 𝗴𝗲𝘁𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗿𝗲𝗮𝗹𝗹𝘆 𝗴𝗼𝗼𝗱 𝗮𝘁 𝗿𝗲𝘄𝗮𝗿𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗷𝗲𝗰𝘁𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗹𝗼𝗼𝗸 𝗶𝗺𝗽𝗿𝗲𝘀𝘀𝗶𝘃𝗲 𝗶𝗻𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗮𝗱 𝗼𝗳 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗷𝗲𝗰𝘁𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝗮𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗮𝗹𝗹𝘆 𝗱𝗶𝗳𝗳𝗶𝗰𝘂𝗹𝘁 𝘁𝗼 𝗯𝘂𝗶𝗹𝗱.

A beautiful landing page.

Fancy animations.

A polished demo.

Those get thousands of likes.

Meanwhile, someone spends months designing a distributed system, building infrastructure, solving concurrency issues, making deployments resilient, and thinking through countless architectural trade-offs...

...and all they have to show is a terminal window and an architecture diagram.

The irony?

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗵𝗮𝗿𝗱𝗲𝗿 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗲𝗻𝗴𝗶𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗯𝗹𝗲𝗺, 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝘄𝗼𝗿𝘀𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗱𝗲𝗺𝗼 𝘂𝘀𝘂𝗮𝗹𝗹𝘆 𝗶𝘀.

---

That's why I'm making a change.

I'm 𝗻𝗼𝘁 building another side project for a while.

Instead, I'm investing that time into documenting the projects I've already built.

Not another landing page.

Not another flashy demo.

I want to document the engineering itself.

• 🏗️ Why the architecture looks the way it does.
• ⚖️ The trade-offs behind every major decision.
• 🔄 How services communicate.
• 🚀 Deployment and infrastructure.
• 📈 Scaling strategies.
• 🔍 What broke, what didn't, and what I'd do differently.

Because I think we've normalized judging engineering by the quality of its presentation instead of the quality of its design.

A great demo tells you 𝘄𝗵𝗮𝘁 a project does.

A great engineering write-up tells you 𝘄𝗵𝘆 it was built that way.

Personally, I find the second one far more interesting.

𝗠𝗮𝘆𝗯𝗲 𝗶𝘁'𝘀 𝘁𝗶𝗺𝗲 𝘄𝗲 𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗿𝘁𝗲𝗱 𝘀𝗵𝗼𝘄𝗰𝗮𝘀𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗲𝗻𝗴𝗶𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴, 𝗻𝗼𝘁 𝗷𝘂𝘀𝘁 𝗺𝗮𝗿𝗸𝗲𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗶𝘁.

𝗦𝘁𝗼𝗽 𝗳𝗲𝗲𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗶𝗿𝗲 𝗙𝗶𝗴𝗺𝗮 𝗳𝗶𝗹𝗲𝘀 𝘁𝗼 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗔𝗜 𝗰𝗼𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗮𝗴𝗲𝗻𝘁𝘀. 𝗬𝗼𝘂'𝗿𝗲 𝗯𝘂𝗿𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘁𝗼𝗸𝗲𝗻𝘀 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗿𝘂𝗶𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗰𝗼𝗱𝗲𝗯𝗮𝘀𝗲.

Like many, I initially treated AI coding tools as magic wands. I’d connect the Figma MCP, point the agent to my data sources, feed it a full design file, and tell it to build.

The result?
A massive 1-2 million token context burn per run. The AI constantly messed up my project-specific API structures, failed to reuse existing components, and generated code that stood out like a sore thumb. Maintaining it manually became a nightmare.

I briefly reverted to writing 80% of the code myself, creating hard-coded skeletons, and only using AI for data integration. But that was painfully slow.

I realized the problem wasn't the AI—it was my workflow. AI agents need structure, tool-composition, and boundaries. Here is the architecture I built to solve this:

𝟭. 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝟰𝟬𝟬-𝗟𝗶𝗻𝗲 𝗠𝗮𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗿 𝗚𝘂𝗶𝗱𝗲𝗹𝗶𝗻𝗲

Instead of letting the agent blindly explore the whole project to understand the context, I used a heavy model (Sonnet/Opus) to analyze the project once. I had it generate a strict 400-line guideline detailing exactly how components should be structured, where imports live, and how our APIs are handled. Now, the agent only reads this doc. Massive token savings.

𝟮. 𝗠𝗶𝗰𝗿𝗼-𝗖𝗼𝗺𝗽𝗼𝗻𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗺𝗽𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴

I stopped feeding the agent entire screens. I put my architect hat on, broke down the UI logically into distinct files, and fed the AI specific sections. I define the exact props and placement, let it build the isolated component, and stitch them together later.

𝟯. 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗔𝘂𝘁𝗼𝗺𝗮𝘁𝗲𝗱 "𝗔𝗱𝗱 𝗜𝗰𝗼𝗻" 𝗦𝗸𝗶𝗹𝗹

AI is notoriously bad at handling icons—hallucinating them or duplicating SVGs. Doing it manually took 1-2 minutes per icon. So, I built a custom automated skill:

• 𝗦𝗰𝗿𝗶𝗽𝘁 𝟭: Uses Figma MCP to extract the exact SVG from the design.
• 𝗦𝗰𝗿𝗶𝗽𝘁 𝟮: Runs it through an SVGR CLI tool to convert it to a React component, then uses a JSCodeShift codemod to instantly convert it into a React Native component.
Now, I just tell the agent, "𝘜𝘴𝘦 𝘵𝘩𝘦 𝘪𝘤𝘰𝘯 𝘴𝘬𝘪𝘭𝘭." Done in seconds.

𝟰. 𝗧𝗵𝗲 "𝗔𝗱𝗱 𝗦𝗰𝗿𝗲𝗲𝗻" 𝗦𝗰𝗮𝗳𝗳𝗼𝗹𝗱𝗲𝗿

I created a dedicated skill that strictly dictates where to register a new screen, where the files go, and how its internal components are managed.

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗧𝗮𝗸𝗲𝗮𝘄𝗮𝘆

By structuring the project correctly and creating the right agentic tools, I don't even need heavy models anymore. I'm currently running this entire workflow on smaller, faster models like DeepSeek v4 Flash. Even with these lighter models, I'm getting significantly more work done—and writing vastly more maintainable code—than I ever did relying purely on the raw power of Sonnet or Opus without guidelines.

Better architecture beats bigger models. Build the right tools, and the AI will do the rest. 🛠️

𝗟𝗶𝗻𝗸𝗲𝗱𝗜𝗻’𝘀 “𝗧𝗼𝗽 𝗔𝗽𝗽𝗹𝗶𝗰𝗮𝗻𝘁” 𝗯𝗮𝗱𝗴𝗲 𝗶𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗯𝗶𝗴𝗴𝗲𝘀𝘁 𝗹𝗶𝗲 𝗶𝗻 𝘁𝗲𝗰𝗵 𝗿𝗲𝗰𝗿𝘂𝗶𝘁𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗿𝗶𝗴𝗵𝘁 𝗻𝗼𝘄.

And it’s exactly why your job search feels like a black hole.

---

Let’s be honest about how job boards actually work. 
They are not optimized to get you hired. They are optimized to generate 𝘢𝘱𝘱𝘭𝘪𝘤𝘢𝘵𝘪𝘰𝘯 𝘷𝘰𝘭𝘶𝘮𝘦. 

Every time a platform tells you:
> “You match 4 out of 10 skills!”
> “You’re a strong fit for this role!”

…it’s just a nudge to keep you clicking. They won't tell you that the 6 missing skills are hard deal-breakers. They won't tell you that the seniority mismatch will get your resume tossed in 0.5 seconds. 

They are heavily biased toward 𝘦𝘯𝘤𝘰𝘶𝘳𝘢𝘨𝘦𝘮𝘦𝘯𝘵 because your clicks drive their metrics.

The result is predictable and exhausting:
• You mass-apply to everything.
• You get ghosted 99% of the time.
• You burn out, blaming your own worth instead of a fundamentally broken system.

---

𝗪𝗲 𝗱𝗼𝗻’𝘁 𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗱 𝗺𝗼𝗿𝗲 𝗳𝗮𝗹𝘀𝗲 𝗵𝗼𝗽𝗲. 𝗪𝗲 𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗱 𝗰𝗹𝗮𝗿𝗶𝘁𝘆.

We need a system that is actually willing to say: 
> “𝘋𝘰 𝘯𝘰𝘵 𝘢𝘱𝘱𝘭𝘺 𝘧𝘰𝘳 𝘵𝘩𝘪𝘴. 𝘠𝘰𝘶 𝘢𝘳𝘦 𝘯𝘰𝘵 𝘢 𝘧𝘪𝘵.”

So I built 𝗝𝗼𝗯 𝗚𝗲𝗻𝗶𝗲. 

It’s not another “auto-apply” AI wrapper that spams 1,000 recruiters with garbage. 
It is a brutal reality check for your job search. 

Drop your resume and paste a job link. Instead of vague cheerleading, Job Genie gives you the raw truth before you waste your emotional energy:

• 𝗔 𝗥𝗲𝗮𝗹 𝗙𝗶𝘁 𝗦𝗰𝗼𝗿𝗲: Good Match, Maybe, or 𝗡𝗼𝘁 𝗮 𝗙𝗶𝘁. (Yes, it will actually tell you not to bother).
• 𝗦𝗸𝗶𝗹𝗹 𝗠𝗮𝘁𝗰𝗵 𝗕𝗿𝗲𝗮𝗸𝗱𝗼𝘄𝗻: The exact hard gaps you are missing, so you know exactly why you might be rejected.
• 𝗔𝗧𝗦 𝗥𝗲𝗮𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆 𝗖𝗵𝗲𝗰𝗸: What the automated filters will actually see when you hit submit.

---

𝗜𝗳 𝗶𝘁’𝘀 𝗮 𝗯𝗮𝗱 𝗳𝗶𝘁?
You know immediately. You close the tab and save your time.

𝗜𝗳 𝗶𝘁’𝘀 𝗮 𝗴𝗿𝗲𝗮𝘁 𝗳𝗶𝘁?
Job Genie instantly tailors your resume and generates a highly targeted cover letter, ready to export as a PDF so you can apply with actual confidence.

---

Your data stays yours. No training AI models, no selling data, no tracking. Just a tool built to help you stop guessing.

Job Genie is in 𝗣𝘂𝗯𝗹𝗶𝗰 𝗕𝗲𝘁𝗮 right now and completely 𝗳𝗿𝗲𝗲 𝘁𝗼 𝘂𝘀𝗲. 

Stop letting job boards farm you for engagement metrics. 
Know your exact fit before you ever click apply.

Try it, break it, and tell me what you think: https://lnkd.in/gsY2NjbC

#𝗝𝗢𝗕𝗦𝗘𝗔𝗥𝗖𝗛 #𝗦𝗢𝗙𝗧𝗪𝗔𝗥𝗘𝗘𝗡𝗚𝗜𝗡𝗘𝗘𝗥𝗜𝗡𝗚 #𝗧𝗘𝗖𝗛𝗖𝗔𝗥𝗘𝗘𝗥𝗦 #𝗦𝗧𝗔𝗥𝗧𝗨𝗣𝗦 #𝗕𝗨𝗜𝗟𝗗𝗜𝗡𝗣𝗨𝗕𝗟𝗜𝗖 #𝗔𝗜

Most people use AI to 𝘥𝘰 𝘵𝘩𝘦𝘪𝘳 𝘸𝘰𝘳𝘬 𝘧𝘢𝘴𝘵𝘦𝘳.

I use AI to build systems that make work get done 𝘣𝘦𝘵𝘵𝘦𝘳.

That’s the difference.

AI is not just a chatbot you prompt. It becomes powerful when you give it:

• access to real environments
• tools to execute and observe
• context beyond just text

• Which requirement is missing?
• Is it a hard constraint or a preference?

These systems are optimized to increase 𝘢𝘱𝘱𝘭𝘪𝘤𝘢𝘵𝘪𝘰𝘯𝘴, not to help candidates make good decisions.

I think job tools should do the opposite.

Clarity > encouragement.

It was annoying. And repetitive.

So I decided to fix it the simplest way I knew how.

I built a small app that converts markdown-style content into 𝗟𝗶𝗻𝗸𝗲𝗱𝗜𝗻-𝗳𝗿𝗶𝗲𝗻𝗱𝗹𝘆 𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗺𝗮𝘁𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴.
Nothing fancy.
No complex logic.

It took me ~𝟮𝟬 𝗺𝗶𝗻𝘂𝘁𝗲𝘀 𝘁𝗼 𝗯𝘂𝗶𝗹𝗱 𝘂𝘀𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘃𝟬.

But while building it, I realized something deeper.

𝗖𝗼𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗶𝘀 𝗻𝗼𝘁 𝗮𝗯𝗼𝘂𝘁 𝘁𝘆𝗽𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗰𝗼𝗱𝗲 𝗶𝗻𝘁𝗼 𝗮𝗻 𝗲𝗱𝗶𝘁𝗼𝗿.
Coding is a 𝗺𝗲𝗮𝗻𝘀 𝘁𝗼 𝗮𝗻 𝗲𝗻𝗱.

That “end” can look very different for different people:

• For some, coding is a form of 𝘀𝗲𝗹𝗳-𝗲𝘅𝗽𝗿𝗲𝘀𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻
• For some, it’s about 𝗰𝗿𝗲𝗮𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗶𝘁𝘆
• For some, it’s about 𝗯𝘂𝘀𝗶𝗻𝗲𝘀𝘀 𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗱𝘀
• For me, it’s about 𝗿𝗲𝗺𝗼𝘃𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗳𝗿𝗶𝗰𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 — making life easier for myself and others

If a small script saves me 15 minutes every day, that’s real value.

Sometimes the best software isn’t revolutionary.

𝗜𝘁 𝗷𝘂𝘀𝘁 𝗾𝘂𝗶𝗲𝘁𝗹𝘆 𝗴𝗶𝘃𝗲𝘀 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝘁𝗶𝗺𝗲 𝗯𝗮𝗰𝗸.


Try out the app here: https://lnkd.in/gn9WBaR4

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗔𝗜/𝗠𝗟 𝘀𝗽𝗮𝗰𝗲 𝗳𝗲𝗲𝗹𝘀… 𝗮 𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘁𝗹𝗲 𝘄𝗲𝗶𝗿𝗱 𝗿𝗶𝗴𝗵𝘁 𝗻𝗼𝘄.

A few years ago, if you wanted to build something like a 𝗯𝗮𝗻𝗸 𝗦𝗠𝗦 𝘁𝗿𝗮𝗻𝘀𝗮𝗰𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗽𝗮𝗿𝘀𝗲𝗿, the approach was very deliberate:

• A classifier to identify whether it’s a transaction SMS
• A bank identifier
• Pattern-based or ML extraction logic per bank
• Lightweight models (CNNs, spaCy, rules) optimized for speed and cost

It wasn’t glamorous — but it was 𝘦𝘯𝘨𝘪𝘯𝘦𝘦𝘳𝘦𝘥.

Today, the default answer has become:
👉 “𝘑𝘶𝘴𝘵 𝘴𝘦𝘯𝘥 𝘪𝘵 𝘵𝘰 𝘢𝘯 𝘓𝘓𝘔.”

Yes, it works.
But it’s also 𝗲𝘅𝗽𝗲𝗻𝘀𝗶𝘃𝗲, 𝘄𝗮𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗳𝘂𝗹, 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗼𝗳𝘁𝗲𝗻 𝘂𝗻𝗻𝗲𝗰𝗲𝘀𝘀𝗮𝗿𝘆.

What bothers me isn’t LLM usage — it’s 𝗵𝗼𝘄 𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘁𝗹𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗼𝘂𝗴𝗵𝘁 𝗴𝗼𝗲𝘀 𝗶𝗻𝘁𝗼 𝘀𝘆𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗺 𝗱𝗲𝘀𝗶𝗴𝗻 𝗻𝗼𝘄.

A more sustainable approach could look like this:

• Use an LLM 𝗼𝗻𝗰𝗲 to generate a 𝗺𝗲𝘀𝘀𝗮𝗴𝗲 𝘁𝗲𝗺𝗽𝗹𝗮𝘁𝗲 / 𝘀𝘁𝗿𝘂𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲
• Store that template as an embedding
• For new messages:
  • Retrieve the closest matching templates from a vector DB
  • Extract values using deterministic logic
• Result: faster execution, edge-friendly, cheaper, and far more scalable

LLMs should be 𝗮𝗿𝗰𝗵𝗶𝘁𝗲𝗰𝘁𝘀, not 𝗱𝗮𝗶𝗹𝘆 𝗹𝗮𝗯𝗼𝗿𝗲𝗿𝘀.

But somewhere along the way, we stopped optimizing.
We stopped asking “𝘊𝘢𝘯 𝘵𝘩𝘪𝘴 𝘳𝘶𝘯 𝘰𝘯 𝘵𝘩𝘦 𝘦𝘥𝘨𝘦?”
We stopped caring about 𝘭𝘢𝘵𝘦𝘯𝘤𝘺, 𝘤𝘰𝘴𝘵, 𝘢𝘯𝘥 𝘭𝘰𝘯𝘨-𝘵𝘦𝘳𝘮 𝘮𝘢𝘪𝘯𝘵𝘢𝘪𝘯𝘢𝘣𝘪𝘭𝘪𝘵𝘺.

This isn’t an anti-LLM post.
It’s a reminder that 𝗴𝗼𝗼𝗱 𝗲𝗻𝗴𝗶𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗱𝗶𝗱𝗻’𝘁 𝗱𝗶𝘀𝗮𝗽𝗽𝗲𝗮𝗿 𝗷𝘂𝘀𝘁 𝗯𝗲𝗰𝗮𝘂𝘀𝗲 𝗽𝗼𝘄𝗲𝗿𝗳𝘂𝗹 𝗺𝗼𝗱𝗲𝗹𝘀 𝗮𝗿𝗿𝗶𝘃𝗲𝗱.

The future isn’t 𝘓𝘓𝘔 𝘦𝘷𝘦𝘳𝘺𝘸𝘩𝘦𝘳𝘦.
It’s 𝗟𝗟𝗠𝘀 𝘄𝗵𝗲𝗿𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗲𝘆 𝗺𝗮𝘁𝘁𝗲𝗿, 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝘀𝘆𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗺𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗸 𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗿𝘆𝘄𝗵𝗲𝗿𝗲 𝗲𝗹𝘀𝗲.

Would love to hear how others are balancing this in real-world systems.

𝗜 𝘄𝗮𝘀𝗻’𝘁 𝘀𝘂𝗿𝗲 𝘄𝗵𝗲𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗿 𝘁𝗼 𝘀𝗵𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝘀, 𝗯𝘂𝘁 𝗜’𝗺 𝘁𝗿𝘆𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘁𝗼 𝗯𝗲 𝗺𝗼𝗿𝗲 𝗼𝗽𝗲𝗻 𝗮𝗯𝗼𝘂𝘁 𝘄𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗜 𝗯𝘂𝗶𝗹𝗱.

I usually avoid posting about personal projects, especially ones that come from uncomfortable experiences. I also don’t enjoy dramatic storytelling, so I’ll keep this simple.

A couple of years ago, I had two hiking trips where I realized I wasn’t as prepared as I thought.

Once, I got stuck at around 𝟱,𝟬𝟬𝟬 𝗳𝗲𝗲𝘁 during a sudden storm. The temperature dropped close to 𝟰°𝗖, and there was no signal. I eventually got out safely, but it stayed with me.

Another time, during a hike on unfamiliar terrain, we lost our way while climbing down. The ascent looked straightforward — just a grassy slope — but on the way back we ended up inside a dense forest with no visible trail. We knew the direction of the exit, but couldn’t find a path. It took a few hours, following a river, to get out.

Nothing dramatic happened in the end.
But those moments made me more careful.

So I built a small app for myself called 𝗧𝗿𝗮𝗶𝗹𝗯𝗹𝗮𝘇𝗲𝗿 (earlier called Travelogram).

It’s a 𝗳𝘂𝗹𝗹𝘆 𝗼𝗳𝗳𝗹𝗶𝗻𝗲 𝗚𝗣𝗦 𝗿𝗲𝗰𝗼𝗿𝗱𝗲𝗿 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗽𝗮𝘁𝗵 𝘁𝗿𝗮𝗰𝗲𝗿.
It records where you go and lets you retrace your steps later — even when there’s no network.

There are 𝗻𝗼 𝘀𝗶𝗴𝗻-𝘂𝗽𝘀, 𝗻𝗼 𝗮𝗻𝗮𝗹𝘆𝘁𝗶𝗰𝘀, 𝗻𝗼 𝘁𝗿𝗮𝗰𝗸𝗶𝗻𝗴.
Your trails stay on your phone.

If you choose to share a trail, it’s just a file — via apps like WhatsApp, or even over Bluetooth if there’s no internet.

---

I’ve been using this quietly for a while now.
It’s not a big launch or a startup pitch.

I’m sharing it because I genuinely believe tools like this can help people travel with a little more confidence — and maybe help someone find their way back to the people waiting for them.

Exploration should end with stories, not worry.
With memories, not close calls.

If Trailblazer helps even one person see their loved ones again, that’s more than enough for me.

Link’s here if you want to take a look: https://lnkd.in/gmtsmYgU

𝟱,𝟬𝟬𝟬+ 𝗵𝗼𝘂𝗿𝘀 𝗯𝘂𝗶𝗹𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗱𝘂𝗰𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝘀𝘆𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗺𝘀 — 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗔𝗜 𝗵𝗮𝘀𝗻’𝘁 𝗰𝗵𝗮𝗻𝗴𝗲𝗱 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗵𝗮𝗿𝗱 𝗽𝗮𝗿𝘁𝘀.

I recently crossed 5,089 hours of active coding since starting my professional journey in 2021.

With all the AI hype, I often hear:
“𝘞𝘪𝘭𝘭 𝘈𝘐 𝘳𝘦𝘱𝘭𝘢𝘤𝘦 𝘦𝘯𝘨𝘪𝘯𝘦𝘦𝘳𝘴?”
“𝘐𝘴 𝘤𝘰𝘥𝘪𝘯𝘨 𝘣𝘦𝘤𝘰𝘮𝘪𝘯𝘨 𝘪𝘳𝘳𝘦𝘭𝘦𝘷𝘢𝘯𝘵?”

After building real systems for half a decade — my answer is simple:
𝗔𝗜 𝗱𝗼𝗲𝘀𝗻’𝘁 𝗿𝗲𝗺𝗼𝘃𝗲 𝗿𝗲𝘀𝗽𝗼𝗻𝘀𝗶𝗯𝗶𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆.

The hardest parts of engineering were never about typing code:
• Designing systems that don’t collapse under load
• Debugging failures you can’t reproduce locally
• Making trade-offs between cost, reliability, and speed
• Owning downtime when users are affected

AI helps me move faster today — but only because those 5,000 hours taught me:
• Where systems break
• What not to automate
• Why reliability matters more than cleverness
• And why “just make it work” always fails at scale

I actively use AI while building backend systems, real-time infrastructure, and automation platforms — 𝗯𝘂𝘁 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗵𝘂𝗺𝗮𝗻 𝗷𝘂𝗱𝗴𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗹𝗮𝘆𝗲𝗿 𝗶𝘀 𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗹𝗹 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗺𝗼𝘀𝘁 𝗰𝗿𝗶𝘁𝗶𝗰𝗮𝗹 𝗽𝗮𝗿𝘁 𝗼𝗳 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗰𝗸.

Tools can generate code.
Only engineers carry production responsibility.

That distinction hasn’t changed.

#SoftwareEngineering #DistributedSystems #BackendEngineering #Infrastructure #SystemDesign #CareerGrowth

Grateful for an incredibly inspiring weekend with the Chennai Web Engineering community 🙌

It was an absolute honor to be one of the speakers at the Agentic AI Mega Event, hosted at HTC Global Services. The energy in the room, the depth of questions, and the curiosity from the audience made this session truly special.

Sharing insights from Artemis—from real-world architecture patterns to the scaling challenges behind building practical AI assistants—was deeply rewarding. What stood out most was how engaged everyone was, not just about what agentic AI is, but how we as engineers can responsibly build and scale these systems in the real world.

A special and heartfelt thanks to Jothi Sankar N Kanagavel — for reaching out, believing in my work, and creating this opportunity for me. Truly grateful for the trust and support 🙏

Big thanks as well to:

 • Chennai Web Engineering for fostering such a strong, learning-driven community
 • HTC Global Services for the warm hospitality and seamless hosting
 • Fellow speakers Bharath Devanathan and Gayathri Perumal for the insightful sessions and shared perspectives
 • Every attendee who showed up curious, asked thoughtful questions, and pushed the conversation forward

Events like these remind me why community matters—learning together, challenging ideas, and growing collectively.

Looking forward to more deep dives, hands-on sessions, and meaningful conversations ahead 🚀
Grateful to be part of this journey.

content:
Most companies are building personal AI assistants the 𝘄𝗿𝗼𝗻𝗴 𝘄𝗮𝘆.

The focus today is on:
“Book me an Uber.”
“Create a Spotify playlist.”
“Do everything on my computer.”

But let’s be honest —
If I have to wait 𝟮𝟬 𝗺𝗶𝗻𝘂𝘁𝗲𝘀 for an Uber, what real difference does it make if I spend 𝟮 𝗲𝘅𝘁𝗿𝗮 𝗺𝗶𝗻𝘂𝘁𝗲𝘀 booking it myself?

And if I truly don’t even have 2 minutes to spare… I probably already have a car and a driver.

Instead of chasing flashy automation, companies should first ask:
𝗪𝗵𝗼 𝗶𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝗮𝘀𝘀𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗻𝘁 𝗮𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗮𝗹𝗹𝘆 𝗳𝗼𝗿?
And more importantly:
𝗜𝘀 𝗶𝘁 𝗶𝗺𝗽𝗿𝗼𝘃𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗾𝘂𝗮𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆 𝗼𝗳 𝗮 𝗽𝗲𝗿𝘀𝗼𝗻’𝘀 𝗹𝗶𝗳𝗲?

For me, my assistant (Artemis) doesn’t book cabs or order food.

It helps me:

 • Eat better and track my nutrition
 • Plan workouts based on yesterday’s meals
 • Get suggestions when I miss key nutrients
 • Practice with a LeetCode question every alternate day
 • Track my expenses and actually 𝘀𝗲𝗲 where my money goes

Could I use separate apps for all this? Absolutely.
Some of them would even do a better job individually.

But here’s the real problem:
𝗗𝗶𝘀𝗰𝗶𝗽𝗹𝗶𝗻𝗲 𝗶𝘀 𝗵𝗮𝗿𝗱. 𝗠𝗮𝗻𝗮𝗴𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗺𝘂𝗹𝘁𝗶𝗽𝗹𝗲 𝗮𝗽𝗽𝘀 𝗶𝘀 𝗵𝗮𝗿𝗱𝗲𝗿. 𝗔𝗻𝗱 𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗿𝘆 𝗲𝘅𝘁𝗿𝗮 𝗮𝗽𝗽 𝗶𝗻𝗰𝗿𝗲𝗮𝘀𝗲𝘀 𝗰𝗼𝗴𝗻𝗶𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗲 𝗹𝗼𝗮𝗱.

With a simple 𝘃𝗼𝗶𝗰𝗲 𝗻𝗼𝘁𝗲 𝗮𝘁 𝗻𝗶𝗴𝗵𝘁, Artemis logs everything for me — health, fitness, finance, learning.

And that has genuinely improved my quality of life.

The goal of a personal assistant shouldn’t be to let users slack off.
It should 𝗲𝗻𝗮𝗯𝗹𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗺 𝘁𝗼 𝗯𝗲𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗲 𝗯𝗲𝘁𝘁𝗲𝗿.

That’s real assistance.

The Chennai Web Engineering community is thrilled to host two incredible sessions focusing on the next evolution of AI and software architecture. Get ready to go Beyond the Chatbot!

Register here: https://luma.com/lma4uoc7

🎤 **Meet Your Speakers & Topics**:
👨‍💻 HARISH KUMAR - Frontend by Profession, Techie by Obsession
Topic: Beyond the Chatbot: Architecture of a Personal AI OS

Harish is diving into the critical architectural challenges developers face when moving from simple chatbots to complex, multi-user AI operating systems.

👩‍🔬 GAYATHRI PERUMAL - AI enthusiast and Mobile Engineer

Topic: Building Better Agents: Mastra AI, Adoption Patterns and GenAI Best Practices

From theory to practice: Gayathri will share her expertise on leveraging GenAI to build powerful, effective agents and the best practices for enterprise adoption.

✨ Don't Miss Out!
Whether you're a seasoned engineer, an AI enthusiast, or just curious about the future of tech, these talks are guaranteed to spark new ideas and provide actionable insights.


Smriti Pradhan Meiyappan Kannappa Sudha Thamotharan Purushothaman Rajaram Gayathri Perumal Harish Kumar K
#ChennaiWebEngineering #WebDev #AI #ArtificialIntelligence #GenAI #TechTalk #HTCGlobalServices #HTCNXT #FrontendEngineering #TechCommunity
Balachandar Natarajan  KAMALESH D  Clarence S Preethi Rajaraman

🔥 𝗧𝗲𝗮𝗰𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗔𝗿𝘁𝗲𝗺𝗶𝘀 𝘁𝗼 𝗧𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗸 𝗶𝗻 𝗖𝗼𝗱𝗲

Just shipped something crazy: 𝗖𝗼𝗺𝗽𝘂𝘁𝗲𝗙𝗹𝗼𝘄 — and it fixes one of the biggest headaches I’ve had with AI agents.

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗯𝗹𝗲𝗺:
My assistant (Artemis) sits on top of thousands of entries — spending logs, workouts, meal tracking, habit data, everything.

When I ask, “𝗛𝗼𝘄 𝗺𝘂𝗰𝗵 𝗱𝗶𝗱 𝗜 𝘀𝗽𝗲𝗻𝗱 𝗼𝗻 𝘁𝗲𝗮 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝗺𝗼𝗻𝘁𝗵?”
…should it really shove 𝟭𝟬,𝟬𝟬𝟬 𝗿𝗼𝘄𝘀 into the LLM context?

Absolutely not.
It's slow, expensive, and borderline stupid.

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗙𝗶𝘅:
With 𝗖𝗼𝗺𝗽𝘂𝘁𝗲𝗙𝗹𝗼𝘄, Artemis now writes small JavaScript snippets on the fly.

Here’s the new workflow:

For the past few months, I’ve been building a personal AI assistant on Telegram - something that helps me plan my day, manage tasks, automate routines, and generally make life a bit smoother.

But I’ve always felt one big limitation:
Most of the data sits in one giant bucket.
Some is in context.
Some is in vector DB.
Nothing is truly structured or isolated.

This makes it tough for the AI to deeply understand a specific part of my life - like my eating habits vs. my workouts vs. my financial planning.

So I built something new.
Still figuring out the name - Skill Matrix or Skill Cortex, but the idea is solid:

🔧 𝗪𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗶𝘀 𝗦𝗸𝗶𝗹𝗹 𝗠𝗮𝘁𝗿𝗶𝘅?

It’s a system that lets the AI create new 𝘀𝗸𝗶𝗹𝗹𝘀, each with their 𝗼𝘄𝗻 𝗺𝗶𝗻𝗶-𝗱𝗮𝘁𝗮𝗯𝗮𝘀𝗲 and 𝘀𝘁𝗿𝘂𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲𝗱 𝗱𝗮𝘁𝗮 𝗺𝗼𝗱𝗲𝗹.

Think of each skill as a small, self-contained “brain module” that knows how to store, process, and reason about one area of my life.

✨ 𝗪𝗵𝘆 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝗶𝘀 𝗮 𝗴𝗮𝗺𝗲-𝗰𝗵𝗮𝗻𝗴𝗲𝗿

Each skill can:

 • Have its own fields and data schema
 • Store structured entries (not just embeddings or free text)
 • Integrate with other skills
 • Build on top of existing automations

For example:

🍽️ 𝗗𝗶𝗲𝘁 𝗧𝗿𝗮𝗰𝗸𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗦𝗸𝗶𝗹𝗹 – Reminds me morning/afternoon/evening to record my meals
🏋️ 𝗘𝘅𝗲𝗿𝗰𝗶𝘀𝗲 𝗦𝗸𝗶𝗹𝗹 – Uses my stored weight + eating patterns to dynamically plan workouts

Suddenly, instead of one overloaded memory blob, I have a scalable system where the AI can reason across skills, combine insights, and make decisions with actual context.

This architecture gives the AI two things:

 • The freedom to compose 𝘀𝗸𝗶𝗹𝗹𝘀 𝘁𝗼𝗴𝗲𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗿 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝘀𝗺𝗮𝗿𝘁𝗲𝗿 𝗿𝗲𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗺𝗲𝗻𝗱𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀

When you build a prototype, you hard-code things, hack things together, and choose whatever works fastest.
That’s the fun part.
But eventually… you have to scale it.

For Artemis, the first version of the reminder system was literally:
“𝙍𝙪𝙣 𝙖 𝙨𝙘𝙝𝙚𝙙𝙪𝙡𝙚𝙧 𝙚𝙫𝙚𝙧𝙮 𝙢𝙞𝙣𝙪𝙩𝙚 𝙖𝙣𝙙 𝙘𝙝𝙚𝙘𝙠 𝙬𝙝𝙤 𝙝𝙖𝙨 𝙖 𝙧𝙚𝙢𝙞𝙣𝙙𝙚𝙧.”
Perfect for a weekend build.
Terrible for scale.

Here’s what I learned while turning that prototype into something production-ready:

🔹 𝟭. 𝗕𝘂𝗶𝗹𝗱 𝗳𝗮𝘀𝘁 𝗳𝗶𝗿𝘀𝘁 — 𝗯𝘂𝘁 𝗶𝘀𝗼𝗹𝗮𝘁𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗹𝗼𝗴𝗶𝗰.
Your future self will thank you when you refactor.

🔹 𝟮. 𝗨𝘀𝗲 𝗾𝘂𝗲𝘂𝗲𝘀 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝗮𝗻𝘆𝘁𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘁𝗶𝗺𝗲-𝗯𝗮𝘀𝗲𝗱 𝗼𝗿 𝗮𝘀𝘆𝗻𝗰𝗵𝗿𝗼𝗻𝗼𝘂𝘀.
RabbitMQ’s delayed queue let me turn a naive loop into a precise, scalable reminder engine.

🔹 𝟯. 𝗠𝗲𝗮𝘀𝘂𝗿𝗲 𝗯𝗲𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗲 𝗼𝗽𝘁𝗶𝗺𝗶𝘇𝗶𝗻𝗴.
I didn’t switch to RabbitMQ until I felt the original approach slowing down.

🔹 𝟰. 𝗥𝗲𝗽𝗹𝗮𝗰𝗲 𝗽𝗶𝗲𝗰𝗲𝘀 𝗼𝗻𝗲 𝗮𝘁 𝗮 𝘁𝗶𝗺𝗲.
A prototype is a living organism — if you change everything at once, it breaks.

🔹 𝟱. 𝗞𝗲𝗲𝗽 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗲𝘅𝗽𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗲𝗻𝗰𝗲 𝗰𝗼𝗻𝘀𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗻𝘁.
Users should feel improvements, not disruption.

Artemis is still growing, and every upgrade teaches me something new about designing assistants that feel human but scale like software.

If you’re building your own project: 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝘁𝗼𝘁𝘆𝗽𝗲 𝗯𝗼𝗹𝗱𝗹𝘆, 𝗿𝗲𝗳𝗮𝗰𝘁𝗼𝗿 𝗽𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗹𝘆.
That’s where the magic happens. ✨

If you’ve known me for a long time, you already know this:
𝗜 𝗻𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗿 𝘂𝘀𝗲𝗱 𝘁𝗼 𝗽𝗼𝘀𝘁 𝗺𝘂𝗰𝗵 𝗼𝗻 𝘀𝗼𝗰𝗶𝗮𝗹 𝗺𝗲𝗱𝗶𝗮. 😅
Not because I wasn’t building - I’ve always spent my weekends making things, trying ideas, and hacking together small projects. I just never shared them.

But recently, that changed.

About two months ago, during a Vagamon trip, I met someone who unknowingly shifted my entire perspective.

We were talking about our work, and as I shared some of the projects I’ve built over the years, he got genuinely excited - more excited than I’ve ever been while talking about my own work.
Then he paused and asked:

“𝗜𝗳 𝘆𝗼𝘂’𝘃𝗲 𝗱𝗼𝗻𝗲 𝗮𝗹𝗹 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝘀... 𝘄𝗵𝗲𝗿𝗲 𝗰𝗮𝗻 𝗜 𝘀𝗲𝗲 𝗶𝘁?”

That question hit different.

Yes, I have GitHub.
Yes, everything is open-source.
But realistically, who is going to dig through a developer’s GitHub to understand their journey?
People don’t browse repositories the way we think they do - and they shouldn’t have to.

That’s when it clicked for me:

As developers, our skills help us do the work.
But if we want to 𝗴𝗿𝗼𝘄, we also need to show the world what we’re capable of.
Visibility isn’t bragging - it’s documentation. It’s storytelling.
It’s how opportunities find you.

I realised I had no social proof of the hundreds of hours I’ve invested into building things I truly enjoyed.
So I decided to change that.

Over the past few weeks, I’ve been sharing more openly - what I’m building, learning, fixing, and creating. And the response has been incredibly encouraging. It’s pushed me to become more consistent, more thoughtful, and more intentional about my work.

If you’re someone who builds quietly… I get it.
But here’s something I’ve learned:

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝘄𝗼𝗿𝗹𝗱 𝗰𝗮𝗻’𝘁 𝗰𝗲𝗹𝗲𝗯𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝘄𝗼𝗿𝗸 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝗻𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗿 𝘀𝗵𝗼𝘄.
𝗔𝗻𝗱 𝘀𝗼𝗺𝗲𝘁𝗶𝗺𝗲𝘀, 𝘀𝗵𝗮𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗶𝘀𝗻’𝘁 𝗮𝗯𝗼𝘂𝘁 𝗮𝘁𝘁𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 - 𝗶𝘁’𝘀 𝗮𝗯𝗼𝘂𝘁 𝗰𝗼𝗻𝗻𝗲𝗰𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻.

So maybe this is your sign to start documenting your journey too.
Share the messy parts, the fun parts, the failed ideas, the weekend hacks - all of it.

You never know who you’ll inspire, help, or meet along the way.

Let’s build loudly, learn publicly, and lift each other up. 🚀

We spent 3.5 months building a P2P system, and then decided to throw it away.!!

When We started WatchWithMe, the goal was simple let people watch together in real time, across the world.

To save infra costs, we went with peer-to-peer (P2P).
We used libraries like simple-peer and others they worked for small sessions, but real users exposed the cracks: random drops, broken ICE negotiations, and peers stuck behind NATs.

So we went all in and built our own P2P library from scratch.
Custom seeding, peer discovery, DHT-style routing, in-browser relays, optimized data channels basically a BitTorrent-inspired WebRTC network.
It took Harish Kumar K almost 2 months to build from scratch and worked beautifully… in perfect conditions.

But P2P depends completely on client networks.
 One weak connection and the whole session broke.
 Fine for file sharing  a disaster for real-time video and audio.
We spent another 1½ months debugging nonstop Interactive Connectivity Establishment(ICE) issues, latency spikes, renegotiations  fixing one thing and breaking two more. We were losing users, time, and sanity.

Then we tried Cloudflare Workers for signaling super fast, globally distributed but too costly so, we took it down after A/B testing shortly.
We had to keep WatchWithMe free for up to 1,000–1,500 users, so it wasn’t sustainable.

Finally, we made the big pivot → LiveKit, an open-source WebRTC SFU.
The migration took time we moved component by component while users stayed live but it paid off.
 ✅ Predictable latency
 ✅ Stable sessions
 ✅ No random drops

We began with one self-hosted server, but as we grew to 4,000+ users across continents, we moved to LiveKit Cloud Subscription for intercontinental reliability.

Yes, there were ups and downs. We lost users along the way. But today, WatchWithMe runs on a rock-solid, scalable infra — and it feels amazing yes we do bare the server + Subscription and other costs.

Best part?
 We’ve done almost no marketing nearly 90% of our users came through word of mouth.

💡 Our takeaway:
Building your own infra teaches you everything, but reliability is what truly earns user trust.

Sometimes the smallest bugs create the biggest storms.

Cloudflare, a company that has stopped some of the largest DDoS attacks in history, recently faced an outage because of… a misplaced React useEffect dependency.

An object recreated on every render tricked React into thinking it was "new," triggering an infinite loop of API calls. The result? A self-inflicted DDoS.

There’s something poetic about a world-class DDoS shield being brought down not by hackers, but by a single line of frontend code.

A reminder for all of us: in engineering, even the tiniest details matter.

How does Chrome work? 
. 
. 
The diagram below shows the architecture of a modern browser. It is based on our understanding of “Inside look at modern web browser” published by the chrome team. 
 
There are in general 4 processes: browser process, renderer process, GPU process, and plugin process. 
 
🔹 Browser process controls address bar, bookmarks, back and forward buttons, etc. 
🔹 Renderer process controls anything inside of the tab where a website is displayed. 
🔹 GPU process handles GPU tasks. 
🔹 Plugin process controls the plugins used by the websites. 
 
The browser process coordinates with other processes. 
 
When Chrome runs on powerful hardware, it may split each service in the browser process into different threads, as the diagram below shows. This is called Servicification.  
 
Now let’s go through the steps when we enter a URL in Chrome. 
 
Step 1: The user enters a URL into the browser. This is handled by the UI thread. 
 
Step 2: When the user hits enter, the UI thread initiates a network call to get the site content. 
 
Steps 3-4: The network thread goes through appropriate network protocols and retrieves the content. 
 
Step 5: When the network thread receives responses, it looks at the first few bytes of the stream. If it is an HTML file, it is passed to the renderer process by the browser process. 
 
Steps 6-9: An IPC is sent from the browser process to the renderer process to commit the navigation. A data pipe is established between the network thread and the renderer process so that the renderer can receive data. Once the browser process hears confirmation that the commit has happened in the renderer process, the navigation is complete and the document loading phase begins.
